coredata.dc:title: One-step synthesis of red emission multifunctional carbon dots for label-free detection of berberine and curcumin and cell imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Meng, Yating
Jiao, Yuan
Zhang, Yuan
Zhang, Huilin
Gong, Xiaojuan
Liu, Yang
Shuang, Shaomin
Dong, Chuan
coredata.dc:description: In this work, the red emission multifunctional carbon dots (R-CDs) were prepared via one-pot hydrothermal strategy of neutral red (NR) and ethylenediamine (EDA) for the label-free detection of berberine and curcumin, cell imaging, and fluorescent flexible film. The as-fabricated R-CDs not only possess good water dispersibility and excellent fluorescence stability, but also were successfully employed as a photoluminescent nanoprobe for label-free monitoring of berberine (BRH) and curcumin (Cur) based on dynamic quenching and internal filter effect (IFE), respectively. More importantly, as-proposed R-CDs displayed outstanding cellular permeability and lower cytotoxicity for cellular applications, which was consistent with the results of confocal fluorescence imaging and cell viability measurement of SMMC7721 cells. Thus, the multifunctional R-CDs may provide a rich tool library for biosensing and cellular imaging reagent applications. Interestingly, R-CDs were also used to manufacture R-CDs/PVA composites as fluorescent flexible films. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first demonstration of a label-free multifunctional fluorescent nanoprobe for berberine and curcumin based on red emission CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Red emission
Multifunctional carbon dots
Label-free probe
Berberine
Curcumin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the fluorescent flexible film

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A)TEM image, HRTEM image (inset); (B) AFM image; (C) FTIR spectrum; (D) Full XPS spectrum; (E) C1s XPS spectrum; (F) N1s XPS spectrum of R-CDs.
(A) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra; (B) Excitation-dependent PL behavior; (C) CIE chromaticity coordinates of R-CDs.

(A) The PL quenching of R-CDs in existence of BRH (0–800 μM), the inset is the SV plot of R-CDs at different concentrations of BRH (30–175 μM). (B) The influence of ions on the PL intensity of R-CDs. (C) UV–vis absorption and (D) Fluorescence decay of R-CDs in the absence and presence of BRH.

(A) The PL quenching of R-CDs in the presence of Cur (0–70 μM), the inset is s the relationship between (F0–F)/F0 and concentration of Cur (0.5–35 μM). (B) The influence of amino acids and drugs on the PL intensity of R-CDs. (C) UV–vis absorption of Cur and excitation and emission spectra of R-CDs. (D) Fluorescence decay of R-CDs in the absence and presence of Cur.
The LSCM images of SMMC7721 cells in the absence (a1-c1) and presence (a2-c2) of BRH.
The photographs of R-CDs/PVA fluorescent flexible film with different content of R-CDs (0%, 1%, 2%, 3% and 4%) under day light, UV light (280 nm) and UV light (365 nm).
Illustration of the synthetic of the multifunctional R-CDs and CDs-based label-free fluorescent nanosprobe for detection of BRH and Cur.
Comparison of the fluorescence behavior of R-CDs with other BRH nanosensors.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: TGA-CdTe QDs = f thioglycolic acid-capped CdTe quantum dots; DNA-AgNCs = DNA-directed AgNCs; CDs = carbon dots.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the fluorescence behavior of R-CDs with other carbon dots based Cur sensors.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: CDs = carbon dots; C-dots = carbon dots; BNCDs = boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots; NP-Cdots = nitrogen and phosphorus dual-doped carbon dots; N,S-CNDs = nitrogen and sulfur dual-doped carbon nanodots; N,Cl-CDs = Nitrogen and chlorine dual-doped carbon nanodots; PNBCDs = P, N, B-co-doped carbon quantum dots; Carbon quantum dots = CQDs.

head.ce:title: One-step synthesis of red emission multifunctional carbon dots for label-free detection of berberine and curcumin and cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Jinzhong 030600, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Jinzhong 030600, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, the red emission multifunctional carbon dots (R-CDs) were prepared via one-pot hydrothermal strategy of neutral red (NR) and ethylenediamine (EDA) for the label-free detection of berberine and curcumin, cell imaging, and fluorescent flexible film. The as-fabricated R-CDs not only possess good water dispersibility and excellent fluorescence stability, but also were successfully employed as a photoluminescent nanoprobe for label-free monitoring of berberine (BRH) and curcumin (Cur) based on dynamic quenching and internal filter effect (IFE), respectively. More importantly, as-proposed R-CDs displayed outstanding cellular permeability and lower cytotoxicity for cellular applications, which was consistent with the results of confocal fluorescence imaging and cell viability measurement of SMMC7721 cells. Thus, the multifunctional R-CDs may provide a rich tool library for biosensing and cellular imaging reagent applications. Interestingly, R-CDs were also used to manufacture R-CDs/PVA composites as fluorescent flexible films. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first demonstration of a label-free multifunctional fluorescent nanoprobe for berberine and curcumin based on red emission CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a zero-dimensional nanomaterial, have attracted tremendous attention due to their advantages in terms of strong photostability [1], favorable biocompatibility [2] and lower cytotoxicity [3], indicating wide utilization in fluorescence sensing [4], bioimaging [5–7] and drug delivery [8–10]. However, most of the reported CDs have maximum blue to green light emission, which restricts their further biological utilization because of severe photodamage of ultraviolet excitation light and obvious blue autofluorescence of biological matrix [11–14]. Meanwhile, red emission light exhibits superior tissue osmotic capability [15–18]. To the best of our knowledge, few simple and reproducible synthetic methods for red emission CDs are reported. Consequently, it is a great challenge to obtain CDs with red emission (see Scheme 1 ).

Berberine (BRH), is the hydrochloride of a kind of natural isoquinolinealkaloid, which is the main active ingredient of several Chinese herbal medicines such as Coptis chinensis and Phellodendron [19,20]. It has drawn immense attention due to its antitumor [21], antituberculosiso [22], antibacterial [23], and antioxidant activities [24] both in vivo and in vitro. Additionally, BRH can also be used as a probe to detect nucleic acid by photoluminescence [25] or resonance light scattering technique [26]. Therefore, it is quite meaningful to sense BRH in biological systems.

Up to now, many analysis methodologies have developed for BRH assay, including fluorescence [26], colorimetric detection [27], fluorophotometry, light scattering (LS) spectrometry [28], High Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) [29], optical fiber sensing [30] and so on. Fluorescence analysis has attracted attention owing to its advantages including good selectivity, high sensitivity, simple equipment and easy operation. Recently, some novel strategies based on fluorescent nanomaterials have been developed to assess BRH. For example, Cao et al. [31] developed a new method for determination of BRH based on static quenching between thioglycolic acid-terminated CdTe quantum dots and BRH. Inspired by this work, the DNA-directed AgNCs was synthesized and utilized as a sensor for BRH detection by BRH-induced DNA aggregation of the content of BRH in the compound BRH tablets [32]. Subsequently, Cheng et al. [33] first established a berberine analysis method based on the blue emission CDs via IFE. However, the blue light damages biomass, which limits its application in the detection of berberine in biological systems. Meanwhile, the long-wavelength CDs can eliminate the interference of berberine's yellow fluorescence on its detection. Therefore, it is imperative to design a facile analytical strategy from long-wavelength CDs for the determination of BRH in biological systems.

Curcumin (Cur) is a natural food pigment extracted from turmeric and other plants, which has anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, anti-indigestion, liver protection, antioxidant and anti-cancer properties [34,35]. However, excessive curcumin has antioxidant activity on DNA and may cause a decrease in intracellular ATP levels and trigger the necrosis process [36,37]. Thus, the development of Cur analysis based on CDs is of important concern. Due to Cur has a strong absorption band at 425 nm, it is easily overlap with the excitation or emission peaks of CDs, so the detection principle of Cur usually attributed to internal filtering effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [38–43]. Unfortunately, the reported CD-based methods of detecting Cur are in short-wavelength region, which limits its application in the biological field. Thus, it is rewarding to develop high-performance Cur anaytsis strategies on the basis of long-wavelength CDs in biomedicine.

Herein, the red emission multifunctional carbon dots (R-CDs) are prepared via one-pot hydrothermal strategy of neutral red (NR) and ethylenediamine (EDA) for the label-free detection of berberine and curcumin, cell imaging, and fluorescent flexible film. The obtained R-CDs were exploited as a label-free nanoprobe for the determination of berberine (BRH) and curcumin (Cur) by a significant fluorescence quenching owing to dynamic quenching and internal filter effect (IFE), respectively. More importantly, the as-fabricated R-CDs can be utilized as an agent for SMMC7721 cells because of its low toxicity and great biocompatibility. Thus, the multifunctional R-CDs may provide a rich tool library for biosensing and cellular imaging reagent applications. Interestingly, R-CDs are also used to manufacture R-CDs/PVA composites as fluorescent flexible films. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first demonstration of a label-free multifunctional nanoprobe for berberine and curcumin based on red emission CDs.
All chemicals, unless otherwise specified, were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich and used as received. Ultrapure water (≥18.25 MΩ.cm) for all experiments was acquired by a Milli-Q Plus system.

In brief, 59 mg of NR, 300 μL of EDA and 20 mL of ultrapure water were loaded into 50 mL of a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and then heated at 200℃ for 5 h. The crude product was centrifuged, dialyzed and lyophilized, and finally the R-CDs powder was obtained.

For BRH detection, 100 μL of R-CDs solution (0.20 mg/ml) was added into 1.9 mL PBS (pH = 7.4) and mixed well. Then, different concentration of BRH were added into the R-CDs solutions, respectively. The PL spectra of the mixture were measured at λex/λem of 474/620 nm. Then, the relationship between the concentration of BRH and PL intensity was fitted by using Origin software.

For the detection of Cur, 100 μL of R-CDs solution (0.20 mg/ml) was added into 1.9 mL PBS, then different contents of 20 μL Cur were mixed with R-CDs solutions, respectively. The PL emission spectra were recorded at λex/λem of 474/620 nm.
The Human hepatic carcinoma SMMC7721 cells were used as model cells to test the cytotoxicity of R-CDs. The experimental procedures were referred to in our previously published papers [44–47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the fluorescent flexible film
ce:section.ce:para: 5 g of Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, molecular weight: 1500) was loaded into 50 mL ultrapure water under vigorous stirring using a magnetic stirrer at 90 °C until a transparent solution was obtained, and then cooled to room temperature. PVA solution was mixed with R-CDs with different contents R-CDs under stirring. The R-CDs solutions were dropped into petri dishes and dried at 60 °C for 1 h. The fluorescent flexible films were obtained.

The structure and morphology of R-CDs are characterized by TEM and AFM. As depicted in Fig. 1 A, R-CDs exhibit exceptional monodispersity with the average size of 2.62 nm. The HRTEM image shows a particular crystal structure with a lattice spacing is 0.21 nm, which corresponding to the (100) facet of graphitic carbon. In addition, AFM image (Fig. 1B) displays that the particle heights of R-CDs is about 2.62 nm. These results demonstrate that R-CDs possess uniform quantum size distribution and excellent dispersion.

FTIR and XPS are conducted to analyze the the surface composition and element of R-CDs. In FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1C), the surface of R-CDs contains OH and NH (3236–3666 cm−1), CH (2896 and 2985 cm−1), CO (1634 cm−1), CN (1402 cm−1) and CO (1060 and 1244 cm−1). As can be seen from Fig. 1D, the XPS survey spectra of R-CDs (Fig. 1D) shows three peaks at 284.2, 400.9 and 532.7 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. The C1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 1E) is dissociated into three major peaks, including sp2 carbons (CC), sp3 carbons (CO/CN) and carboxyl carbons (COOH) at 284.3 eV, 285.4 eV and 286.9 eV, respectively. As shown in Fig. 1F, the N1s band is disassembled into three peaks at 398.9 eV, 399.8 eV and 400.9 eV, which are associated with pyridine-N, amino-N and pyrrolic-N, respectively. The O1s band (Fig.S1B) exhibits two peaks at 530.9 eV for CO and 532.3 eV for CO. In summary, all these observations demonstrate that the R-CDs have amino and carboxyl groups.

The optical properties of R-CDs are probed based on UV–Vis absorption and PL spectroscopy. Fig. 2 A showed that the R-CDs exhibit two absorption peaks at 276 nm and 452 nm, which attributed to π → π* transition of the CC bond and the n → π* transition of the CO and CN bond. The optimal λex and λem of R-CDs are 474 nm and 620 nm (Fig. 2A), respectively. Fig. 2B illustrated that R-CDs solution exhibit excitation wavelength dependent properties. As the excitation wavelength increases, the emission wavelength of R-CDs has a red shift. Chromaticity diagram (CIE) was explored to further investigate the FL emission of R-CDs (Fig. 2C). The CIE coordinates was calculated as (0.6915, 0.3080), which confirms that the as-proposed R-CDs has great potential as a fluorescent probe in biological systems. The QY of R-CDs is calculated as 8.8% (Fig.S2). In addition, the R-CDs solution is under continuously irradiated with 60 min and PL intensity remains invariant, signifying good photostability (Fig.S3). Meanwhile, different pH values (3–12) and high ionic strength have almost no noticeable change for photoluminescence intensity of R-CDs, indicating that R-CDs can be used in biosystems.

The fluorescence of R-CDs showed a gradual decrease in the presence of BRH (Fig. 3 A). The F0/F and BRH contents have an excellent linear relationship from 10 to 175 μM (Fig. 3A inset, y = 0.0025x + 0.0151, R2 = 0.9924). The detection limit is 1.5 μM.

To assess the specificity of R-CDs for BRH assay, the PL spectra in the presence of irons on the photoluminescence of the R-CDs were inspected. Fig. 3B explained that the selectivity of the proposed R-CDs probe for BRH was superior to that for other substances. Compared with other fluorescent BRH sensor based on carbon dots, the R-CDs possess longer fluorescence emission, which make it more valuable for BRH measurements in biological systems in the future (Table 1 ).

In order to elucidate the underlying mechanism of BRH-induced fluorescence quenching, the Stern-Volmer equation was be utilized to analyze the quenching behavior of R-CDs/BRH system:
F 0
/ F - 1 =
K
SV
Q
=
K q
τ 0
Q
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of BRH, respectively; KSV is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant; [Q] is the concentration of BRH; Kq is the quenching rate constant and τ0 is the fluorescence lifetime of the R-CDs. From the inset of Fig. 3A, the Stern-Volmer plot shows a linear response (R2 = 0.9963) and the calibration equation was F0/F = 0.9739 + 0.0045 [BRH]. Dynamic and static quenching effect could not be simply attributed to Stern-Volmer because both of cases fit into the equation.

Additionally, the UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence lifetimes were investigated.As depicted in Fig. 3C, the UV–vis absorption spectra of BRH could overlap perfectly with the difference absorption spectrum between R-CDs/BRH systems and R-CDs at the same wavelength range (200–700 nm), which concluded that no compound is formed between R-CDs and BRH. To further inspect the mechanism of photoluminescence quenching, the fluorescence decay dynamics was calculated. The fluorescence decay curves of R-CDs and R-CDs/BRH can be fitted by a double-exponential formula (Fig. 3D). After adding BRH, the lifetime of the R-CDs increased, which supported the dynamic quenching mechanism. Therefore, the dynamic quenching occurred between R-CDs and BRH.

To further validate the selectivity of Cur sensing, 15 kinds of amino acids and 5 kinds of drugs were used as control samples. Fig. 4B revealed that all of these amino acids and drugs had negligible influence on Cur assay. Thus, the R-CDs is a great potential label-free probe for Cur determination. Compared with other fluorescent method of Cur assay based on carbon dots, R-CDs has a longer fluorescence emission time, which makes it more potentially valuable for Cur analysis in biological systems (Table 2 ).

To further demonstrate the quenching mechanism of Cur, UV–Vis absorption spectra and fluorescence lifetime were measured. As shown in Fig. 4C, the absorption band of Cur was overlapped with the excitation peak of R-CDs, which may cause IFE beteween R-CDs and Cur. Fig. 4D illustrated that in before and after adding Cur, the fluorescence lifetime of R-CDs does not change significantly. Therefore, IFE occurred between R-CDs and Cur.

SMMC7721 cells were selected as model cells by the standard MTT (3-(4,5Dimethylthiazol- yl)-2,5Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5diphenyltetrazolium bromide) assay to exploit the cytotoxicity of R-CDs. As depicted in Fig.S6, more than 80% cells could survive after incubated with different contents of R-CDs (0–400 μg/mL), which indicated that R-CDs possess low toxicity and great biocompatibility.

To investigate the feasibility of R-CDs in biological system, human hepatocarcinomatous SMMC7721 cells are chosen as criteria for evaluating the cytotoxicity of neutral red and R-CDs. As depicted in Fig.S6, the cell viability of R-CDs is higher than neutral red and reach to 80% while incubating SMMC7721 cells with R-CDs from 0 to 400 μg·mL−1, which indicates the superior biocompatibility and ultra-low cytotoxicity of the R-CDs.

To further quest the potential use of R-CDs in living cells, the cellular uptakes of R-CDs were viewed by LSCM. As shown in Fig. 4, R-CDs incubated SMMC7721 cells displayed bright red fluorescence. These cells emitted blue, green, and red (Fig.S7) emissions when they were stimulated by different wavelength lasers, respectively. The merged images of the first and second panels, second and third panels, and first and third panels (Fig.S7d-f) illustrate the excellent ability of R-CDs in permeating the cell membrane. These results indicated that R-CDs can be chosen as an agent for detection of BRH in biomedical system. When BRH was added to CD-stained cells, the red fluorescence intensity decreased. Also, Fig. S8 displayed the fluorescence was quenched after adding Cur. These results signified that the R-CDs were great cellular permeability and capable of monitoring BRH and Cur in living cells.

To confirm the applicability of R-CDs as fluorescent flexible film, PVA was selected to fabricate R-CDs/polymer composites. Fig. 6 (a) is the photographs of R-CDs/PVA film with different R-CDs (0–4%) under day light and UV light. When the content of R-CDs increased, the fluorescence of R-CDs/PVA film increased gradually accompanied. Because R-CDs have excitation wavelength dependence, as the ultraviolet excitation wavelength increases, the fluorescence of the fluorescent composite film gradually red shifts. In brief, the as-fabricated R-CDs were greatly advantageous to fabricate photovoltaic materials.

In summary, we developed the red emission multifunctional carbon dots (R-CDs) as a photoluminescent nanoprobe for label-free monitoring of berberine (BRH) and curcumin (Cur) from dynamic quenching and internal filter effect (IFE), respectively. The R-CDs were prepared via one-pot hydrothermal technique. More importantly, R-CDs were employed as an agent for SMMC7721 cells because of its great biocompatibility and lower toxicity. It is hopeful that R-CDs have promising applications in biosensing and biomedical optical imaging. Interestingly, R-CDs are also used to manufacture R-CDs/PVA composites as fluorescent flexible films. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first demonstration of a label-free nanoprobe for berberine and curcumin based on red emission CDs.

Yating Meng: Resources, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Yuan Jiao: Investigation. Yuan Zhang: Formal analysis. Huilin Zhang: Supervision. Xiaojuan Gong: Project administration, Supervision. Yang Liu: Supervision. Shaomin Shuang: Project administration, Supervision. Chuan Dong: Project administration, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21874087 and 21705101) and Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (2017 Key 1).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.119432.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
